title: Spinning Icelandic dual-coat fibre
subtitle: Separating and spinning the tog and thel layers of Icelandic fleece as a traditional lopi-style yarn
excerpt: Icelandic sheep produce a dual-coat fleece with two distinct fibre types: the long, coarse outer coat (tog) and the short, fine inner layer (thel). Separated and re-blended in different proportions, they produce yarns ranging from rustic outerwear weight to surprisingly soft next-to-skin work. This entry covers both separation and blended spinning.
sourceNotes: Icelandic fleece preparation and dual-coat spinning synthesised from general Icelandic breed spinning practice. The tog-thel separation technique is the basis of traditional lopi yarn production.

glossaryTerms.definition: The long, coarse outer coat of the Icelandic fleece. Tog fibres are lustrous, water-repellent, and suit outerwear applications.
The fine, soft inner coat of the Icelandic fleece. Thel fibres are much softer than tog and can be worn next to skin. Thel is also called 'the wool fibre' in Icelandic textile tradition.
A traditional Icelandic unspun or lightly spun yarn made from the complete dual-coat fleece. Its characteristic air content and insulating properties come from the mixture of long and short fibres.
A spinning method that drafts fibres loosely from a carded preparation, creating a soft, lofty yarn with significant air trapped between fibres.

content.text: Icelandic fleece is distinctive in having two clearly separable fibre types in the same lock. The outer
tog
fibres are long (10-15 cm), lustrous, and coarse; the inner
thel
fibres are much shorter (5-8 cm), soft, and warm. Traditional Icelandic
lopi
blends both layers without separation for maximum loft and insulation. The separated layers can also be spun individually for very different yarns.

attrs.title: Wool dust caution
attrs.body: Carding Icelandic fleece throws fine fibre dust. Work in a ventilated room and damp-mop after. People with asthma should wear a dust mask.

items.name: Raw Icelandic fleece, skirted and washed
Spinning wheel (castle type preferred for lopi-style)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Separate tog and thel

Take a single washed Icelandic lock and hold the cut end (base) in one hand. The tog fibres are the longer, coarser outer fibres and will be on the outside of the lock. Pull the tog layer away from the inner thel fibres by drawing the outer layer upward from the tip end. The thel will remain as a compact, softer bundle in the remaining inner part of the lock. Sort tog and thel into two separate baskets as you work through the fleece.
Step 2: Card the separated layers

Card the thel fibre separately using a medium-tooth hand card. The shorter thel fibres are softer and card into a much finer, fluffier rolag than the tog. Card the tog separately: the longer fibres require fewer card strokes to open but will align more loosely. For a blended
lopi
-style yarn, card tog and thel together in roughly equal proportions without separating: load the card alternately with tog and thel fibres for each rolag.
Step 3: Spin using the woollen draw
Set the wheel to a moderate twist ratio. Use the
woollen draw

for the blended preparation: allow twist to enter the drafting zone and draw back against the twist to create the characteristic lofty, open structure. Traditional lopi-style yarn is spun very loosely with minimal twist, relying on the interlocking of the long tog fibres with the short thel fibres for strength. Do not overspin: the yarn should be barely twisted enough to hold together.
Step 4: Ply or leave as singles

Traditional lopi yarn is sold as unplied singles or very lightly plied. To make a lopi-style singles, spin the blended preparation loosely, wind off onto the niddy-noddy, and set the twist by soaking in warm water for 20 minutes before hanging to dry under light tension. The yarn will be noticeably lighter per length than a plied yarn of the same thickness, and very warm. For a more hardwearing yarn, ply two singles together in the S-direction.

items.problem: The tog and thel layers do not separate cleanly from the lock
items.cause: The fleece has not been fully washed, and lanolin is still holding the layers together
items.fix: Re-wash in very hot water (60°C) with washing-up liquid. Once the lanolin is removed, the layers separate easily.

items.problem: The lopi-style singles break repeatedly while spinning
items.cause: The twist was too low for the drafted thickness
items.fix: Increase the wheel tension slightly and slow the drafting to allow slightly more twist into the yarn before drawing back. Lopi requires just enough twist to hold: test by spinning a length and giving it a firm tug.

items.problem: The finished yarn feels coarse and scratchy even after washing
items.cause: The tog proportion in the blend is too high
items.fix: Increase the thel proportion in future preparations. The thel layer is the soft inner coat; the tog provides strength and lustre but contributes most of the coarseness.
